tax: Terrae Elementalis is placed at species rank beneath Terrae (Lithic order, Earth Elementals class). Diagnosis rests on a roughly humanoid stone body 2–3 m tall with a featureless head-mass, two arm-masses, and a broad stone trunk. It is separated from its sibling Terrae elementalis minor by its mid-sized 2–3 m standard body versus the minor form's compact sub-2 m mass; the two share the genus body-plan but never the the compact sub-2 m form. Composition, not colour or menace, fixes the placement: mixed stone types — basalt, granite, and quartz veins — shaped by tectonic pressure into a bipedal form.
ap: Terrae Elementalis stands 2–3 m and 3–6 tonnes. Its silhouette is a roughly humanoid mass with a rounded head, two distinct arm-masses, and legs that merge below the knee into a broad stone plinth. Three diagnostic surface markers separate it in the field: a featureless smooth head-mass with two deep orbital hollows that glow faint amber in active bodies; arm-masses ending in blunt stone mitten-hands, leaving flat-edged impact marks rather than claw strikes; and a gait that shakes the floor measurably at 10 m, distinguishable from a Galeb Dur's two-step by the double-beat of leg-plinth and body-mass. It moves with a slow powerful stride on a broad base, shaking the floor at 10 m, leaving two plinth-impressions and a body-draught, and shows faint amber glow in the two orbital hollows, visible up to 30 m in dark passages. Even after dispersal it can be identified from two rounded plinth-impressions flanking a central drag-groove from the body-mass, and scored stone where its fists struck, so a survey team need not see the living body to log the species.
eco: Terrae Elementalis persists in fault-veined passages, worked quarry faces, and stone-pressure zones of the mid-lower tiers where tectonic pressure can sustain a large mass. It does not breed; it forms where deep tectonic pressure along a major fault line animates mixed stone into a bipedal form, and recurs whenever the fault re-pressurises and the same volume of stone re-forms at the same site. It sustains its mass on deep tectonic pressure, drawing in loose stone and fault-debris incorporated into the mass. Range is bounded by the fault pressure zone: outside that band the body slows, loses bipedal coherence, and slumps into rubble. Juvenile or nascent forms appear as a fault exposure where stone fragments have been moving upward rather than falling, which lets a surveyor predict where an adult will rise.
beh: Behaviour is the behaviour of its medium given intent. Terrae Elementalis is roused by sustained vibration (drilling, blasting) or a crew crossing its fault zone and otherwise lies an irregular standing stone mass indistinguishable from a pressure-split column. It claims a single fault zone or quarry face and answers intrusion with striking with flat-mitted fists and attempting to push prey into walls or corners. Toward grey-folk it is territorial but predictable; survey crews report that Stonesong Heralds mark fault zones with a three-notch chisel-sign meaning 'standing stone that walks'. It is not cultural and keeps no craft — its 'decisions' are thresholds of pressure, heat, current, or charge, not plans.
traitsText: Stable characters of Terrae Elementalis: a body of mixed stone types — basalt, granite, and quartz veins — shaped by tectonic pressure into a bipedal form; immune to crushing and fire; and a defining weakness — sustained vibration at its natural resonance, which disaggregates the mixed-stone body. Its senses run through the medium itself: tremor sensing through its plinth-base at 50 m, and pressure sensing through the fault it stands on. It tolerates heat, dust, and darkness but fails at sustained resonance and loss of fault pressure. Mass varies with available element, yet the proportions, the 2–3 m bipedal form and amber orbital glow, stay constant, which is why mass alone never reclassifies the taxon.
abilities: Abilities of Terrae Elementalis are bounded by its anatomy, not borrowed from other elements: it strikes with flat-mitted fists, leaving flat-edged bruises rather than cuts; it incorporates loose stone to repair damage on the move; it senses approach through its plinth-base at 50 m before the crew can see it; and it can set itself rigid as natural stone, requiring resonance testing to identify. Each is a direct expression of deep tectonic pressure shaping a mixed-stone bipedal form, so it cannot, for instance, conduct current, fly, cross deep water, or produce flame. The powers scale with body mass and fade as the body disperses.
bg: In the Throat, Terrae Elementalis matters because it is the most frequently encountered large elemental in the worked fault zones, and the three-notch chisel-sign now marks every quarry wall it has been observed on. Grey-folk survey practice treats it as a fault-zone territorial hazard, logged by the Stonesong Heralds before any quarry lease is signed, and its presence reshapes routes through the mid-lower fault passages, now surveyed by tremor-rod before deep drilling begins. The Stonesong Heralds have a classification tradition that the standard Terrae is the 'primary voice' of the fault — the most common body the stone takes when it speaks.
g: Terrae Elementalis (Terrae elementalis) is the standard earth elemental: a roughly humanoid walking mass of living stone 2–3 m tall, the most commonly encountered Terrae form in the gorge. Documented from the fault-veined stone passages and quarry faces of the mid-lower tiers, it expresses a single inner element as its whole anatomy — a humanoid body of mixed stone types — basalt, granite, and quartz — held in rough bipedal form by tectonic pressure — rather than as flesh laid over an animal skeleton. Where the Galeb Dur is a boulder that walks, the Terrae is stone shaped roughly into a person: a mass that has found a convenient bipedal form for crossing passages. Field teams record it as a true elemental, not a beast wearing mineral armour: when it disperses it leaves a scatter of mixed stone rubble in a rough humanoid outline, slowly sinking into the floor and no carcass.
